prompt:
Our snapshot pruner (snapprune package) only knows keep-last-N and storage is drowning: keep-last-30 of hourly DB snapshots is 30 hours of history, not 30 days. Ops signed off on a proper grandfather-father-son policy, and I promised them a dry-run mode after last quarter's incident, so here's the ask.

PlanGFS(names, policy, now) — now is passed in, NOT read from the wall clock, because the nightly job replays plans in tests and postmortems. Policy has Daily, Weekly, Monthly ints, meaning: for each of the last Daily calendar days (counting back from now's UTC date, today included), keep the newest snapshot of that day, reason "daily". Same idea for the last Weekly ISO weeks (current week included) with reason "weekly", and the last Monthly calendar months (current month included, mind the year rollover) with reason "monthly". Buckets are checked daily first, then weekly, then monthly; if a bucket's newest snapshot is already kept it contributes nothing extra — that's the whole GFS thinning effect, don't "fix" it. A snapshot is kept once with the first reason that claimed it; everything unclaimed is deleted.

It returns a Plan: Keep is []Decision{Name, Reason} and Delete is []string, both ordered newest-first with the same name-ascending tie-break the package already uses. Any malformed snapshot name is an error, empty input is an empty plan.

Guard rail: a plan must NEVER delete every snapshot. If no bucket claimed anything, keep the newest snapshot with reason "guard" (so a lone ancient snapshot survives with an empty delete list).

Dry run: Plan.Render() returns one line per decision — "keep <name> (<reason>)" for keeps then "delete <name>" for deletes, each line \n-terminated, empty plan renders as the empty string. The ops runbook will copy-paste this, so the format is exact.

gfs_test.go is the contract. snapprune_test.go pins Timestamp and KeepLastN behavior we still rely on — keep it green, modify neither test file. Verify with go test -race -timeout 30s ./...
